title: dbuilding, understanding equilibria can make economies or political conflicts in
content:
dbuilding, understanding equilibria can make economies or political conflicts in a story more believable – characters will find stable arrangements unless something shifts. Cross-domain, the concept of equilibrium appears in many systems (physical, biological). Nash equilibrium is the game-theoretic analogue of something like a chemical equilibrium (no incentive for molecules to react further given the energy state). It’s a reminder that agents seeking their own best outcome can lead to stable but suboptimal outcomes (hence the need for cooperation or external change to break an equilibrium). As a principle, it encourages analysis of incentives and payoffs in any designed system – a good designer ensures that the equilibrium of player strategies aligns with fun or fair outcomes, otherwise players might exploit or stagnate the game.
• “Funativity” Balance (8 Kinds of Fun) (Domain: Game Design Theory) – In game design, it’s recognized there are multiple types of fun a game can provide (per Marc LeBlanc’s MDA framework and others): e.g., sensation (audio-visual delight), fantasy (make-believe, story), narrative (drama), challenge (gameplay difficulty), fellowship (social fun), discovery (exploration), expression (creativity, customization), submission (mindless pastime). A good game often balances several of these “fun” factors to engage a wide audience. Examples: An MMORPG might combine fellowship (player guilds, raids) with discovery (exploring a vast world), challenge (strategic combat), and fantasy (role-playing as an elf wizard). A party game like Mario Kart offers challenge and competition, but also fellowship (couch multiplayer) and sensation (colorful chaotic action). Some games lean heavily on one kind of fun (a horror game emphasizes sensation and narrative, maybe less on fellowship or expression). Implications: This “eight kinds of fun” list is more taxonomy than law, but it functions like a design checklist law: a robust design often considers multiple engagement types. It’s a reminder of the
